column18:
Town Hall Survey

Informed Consent


Hello Chico Student:


The following online survey is part of an important university study on how to improve students' experiences at the Town Hall Meeting. It will take approximately 5-10 minutes to complete.






















Taking part in this research is completely voluntary. If you choose to participate in this research, it is very important that you answer the questions as thoughtfully and frankly as possible. All your answers will be kept strictly confidential. You can stop participating at any time without penalty to your grade.
We would also like to emphasize that you will NOT be penalized if you choose to omit answers to some questions on the survey. To record your answers, you must proceed all the way through the end of the survey, even if you choose to omit answers to some questions. In addition, proceeding to the very end of the survey will remove any survey response information from the screen on your computer/device, protecting your privacy. 


Your student ID is being collected so we can match your responses to other important university programs (U-Course, Town Hall Meeting, Great Debate) and demographic information. IDs may be used for statistical analysis alongside other data such a GPA, retention, and graduation rates. Again, your answers are strictly confidential, and we are bound by rules of this University to maintain this confidentiality. Your information collected as part of this research will be stored on a password protected computer in a locked office within the First-Year Experience Program office and on a protected server that is located behind a secure firewall at the University Office for Institutional Research. You must be 18-years of age or older to take this survey.


Thank you for filling out this survey as completely and honestly as you can. We are asking you to do this because we value your experience and ideas about how to improve students' experiences here at Chico State.


If you'd like more information about this project, contact the First-Year Experience Program office at fye@csuchico.edu or (530) 898-3705.

column41:
The meeting took too long to start, and the speakers spent too much time talking in the beginning. If the meeting starts at 6, it should start at 6. I feel bad for the people who showed up early. The discussion groups had us check in with a QR code, so maybe that could be applied to the initial check in process. Much of what the speakers talked about was not targeted at the students attending, similar to the empty thank-you speeches given at commencements, and I could visibly see the students around me look completely uninterested the entire time while they looked a their phones. Most students don't want to be at the meeting from the outset, so dragging on the least interesting part in the beginning will make people disengage completely before the meeting starts. All the students in the audience are thinking "Why are we even here?" One speaker emphasized the utility gained from the meeting, and that was the most engaging speech, because it tried to answer that question.

Everyone in my discussion section had the same ideas, and it was a bit of an echo chamber. Discussion felt more like a show-and-tell rather than a discussion. It would've been nice to hear from other policy topic groups as well. I was interested in more than one policy topic, but had to choose only 1.

The end section where we went around to different tables felt like a waste of everyone's time. The system encouraged everyone to grab 3 stamps as quickly as possible in order to leave. The information offered by the experts was extremely bare-bones and not beyond what we already learned in class. I would've rather heard from other discussion groups who picked the same or different policy topic.

column29: That they were not super useful. The opening statements were somewhat interesting, but it felt more like a bunch of speeches than anything else. Little counter-arguments were brought up, with the moderators acting more as article recommendators than anything else. I felt like if the moderators played more devil's advocate, or rather gave less "read this article," and gave more realistic expectations for trying to implement a solution.
column30: Social media for digital advocacy,Petition,Elect supportive public officials
column31: Write a letter to the editor
column41: Make it more of a debate. It felt like a bunch of mini-speeches with little devil's advocate for my breakout room. If there was more devil's advocate, as well as more what-if scenarios based upon the groups positions, I feel that we all could've learned more. Also, trimming the fat on the opening theatrics, and increased the debates would've been beneficial, if the "debates," were done as true debates. Lastly, I would've rather had people who deal with the public system, specifically in the areas of our positions would have been 10x better. In my case, we had two professors giving us more research on each topic, rather than people who live and deal with the current policies and regulations. I felt like in my breakout room, I was the person who was closest to many positions, since I actually work in law enforcement & criminal justice. I felt I was the "expert," in the room, rather than the professors, who brought up little modern examples, or even current policies over "read this research." This all could've been down to my room specifically, but from gathering some public opinions, it was more widespread. As for the symposium, I wish it was more staggered, and more of a "learn something," than the current get-my-stamp-and-dash/wait system. A staggered entry time for each different group could've helped the chaos. The people who were there were knowledgeable, just it felt like they were lacking directions sometimes, and had to wing it for some situations, when they shouldn't have had to.
